Question Text: In this novel, a Harvard graduate is said to know “everything there was to know about literature, except how to enjoy it.” The C.I.D. investigates censored letters signed with the forged signature “Washington Irving” in this novel. A character in this novel sells Egyptian cotton dipped in chocolate as head of (*) M&M Enterprises. An IBM machine in this novel promotes a man, resulting in his name becoming Major Major Major Major. For 10 points, name this Joseph Heller novel in which Yossarian’s attempts to avoid flying missions are prevented by the title paradoxical rule.

A pool boy in this movie is revealed to be gay when he clocks the main character’s shoes as being last season Prada. The main character of this movie says "Whoever said orange was the new pink was seriously disturbed" after introducing her Chihuahua, Bruiser. That main character helps the hairdresser Pauline get her dog back and teaches her the (*) “bend and snap.” This film’s protagonist clashes with Vivienne, the new girlfriend of her ex-boyfriend Warner, and it culminates with the trial of Brooke Wyndham. For 10 points, name this movie starring Reese Witherspoon as CULA sorority president Elle Woods, who goes to Harvard Law School.

This location came into being with the help of research conducted by Harvard professor Gerald Lesser and the “CTW model.” An episode poignantly discussed the death of this location’s resident storekeeper Mr. Hooper, and a character from this location warns against finishing the “terrifying” book (*) The Monster at the End of This Book. To encourage children to report sexual abuse, this location’s resident Mr. Snuffleupagus was proven as real. Shows set in this location are sponsored by numbers and letters of the alphabet. For 10 points, name this fictional location, the residence of Grover, Oscar the Grouch, and Big Bird.

The second narrator of this novel breaks the glass on his father’s watch against the corner of his dresser, but the watch keeps ticking. In addition to time, that character is haunted by streetlamps and Dalton Ames, and he gives Deacon a note to deliver to his roommate at Harvard after he commits suicide. The first narrator of this novel has (*) no sense of time and appreciates that “Caddy smelled like trees”. The third narrator of this novel regrets giving the motherly servant Dilsey permission to go to Easter service. For 10 points, Quentin’s, Benjy’s and Jason’s lives are all affected by Caddy’s promiscuity in what novel by William Faulkner?
Answer: The Sound and the Fury; Sound and the Fury

Question Text: Ben Mezrich alleged that this man ate koala meat on the yacht of a Sun Microsystems executive. After being dumped by a girl from BU who says she likes men who row crew, this man drunkenly uses Livejournal while incorporating his friend's chess-ranking algorithm into a website that allowed users to rank the attractiveness of girls on his campus. While being deposed, he attacked the (*) “Match.com for Harvard guys” that he was hired to develop. He is told to “Drop the 'the'” by Sean Parker, who assists this man in removing Eduardo Saverin from their company's board, and he was sued by the Winkelvoss twins. For 10 points, name this man portrayed by Jesse Eisenberg in The Social Network, who created Facebook.

A book of this name, which contains the chapter “Arguing Affirmative Action” before summarizing Aristotle, adapts a lecture taught by Michael Sandel at Harvard. This quality is undesirable according to Thrasymachus’s second objection. The theoretical “veil of ignorance” is key to A Theory of [THIS] by John Rawls, who defines it as fairness. The ideal city is an analogy for this trait in Plato’s Republic, in which Socrates seeks to define it. For 10 points, name this virtue symbolized by a blind “lady” holding a scale, often equated with people getting what they deserve.

In the movie EuroTrip, a character played by this actor sings the song “Scotty Doesn’t Know.” This actor has received Emmy nominations for co-producing the series Project Greenlight, and has earned Oscar nominations for producing Manchester by the Sea and playing Francois Pienaar in Invictus. This man is often kicked off of (*) Jimmy Kimmel Live!, usually due to lack of time, but later retaliated with a takeover of the show. This Bostonian actor won an Oscar at only 27 years old, for co-writing the screenplay to the film Good Will Hunting with his longtime friend, Ben Affleck. For 10 points, name this actor most famous for playing Jason Bourne.

This poet created a “hermit heiress... in her dotage” who “buys up all the eyesores facing her shore.” A poem dedicated to this man describes “illegal fire balloons” that leave an armadillo “clenched ignorant against the sky.” This poet wrote, “I myself am hell” in a work for his friend Elizabeth Bishop. This poet wrote, “a savage servility slides on by grease” in a poem about a (*) monument to Robert Gould Shaw that “sticks like a fishbone / in the city’s throat” where the Old South Boston Aquarium used to be. For 10 points, name this confessional poet of “Skunk Hour” and “For the Union Dead.”

A Sub-Sub Librarian and a Late Consumptive Usher supply an Etymology and a series of Extracts which preface this work. One character in it declares that a ship was his “Yale College” and his “Harvard.” Another character in this work discovers he can no longer enjoy a pipe and casts it overboard. While other characters in this novel include the harpooners Daggoo, Tashtego, and the cannibal, Queequeg, and the first mate, Starbuck. For 10 points, name this novel, which begins “Call Me Ishmael,” and was written by Herman Melville.

This author of "The Rhodora" and the Harvard address "The American Scholar" wrote that "a foolish consistency is the hobgoblin of little minds" in one essay. This man rented his pond to Henry David Thoreau, a fellow (*) Transcendentalist. For 10 points, name this American author of essays like "The Over-Soul" and "Nature."

A character on this show is constantly reminded of the 146 other things that can be done when a metaphorical gun is put to your head. The season one finale of this show concerns the murder of Clifford Danner’s girlfriend, for which he was wrongfully imprisoned. An American actress portrayed (*) Rachel Zane on this show for seven seasons until her marriage to Prince Harry. On this show, a fraudulent Harvard graduate is among the associates managed by Louis Litt. For 10 points, Mike Ross and Harvey Specter are lawyers on what legal drama?

This artist was portrayed by David Elias Callaham in the 2021 parody film America: The Motion Picture. They were heavily inspired by garden villas and eventually designed a museum near the location of said villas. This architect put his degree from the Harvard University Graduate School of Design into the construction of the (*) John F. Kennedy Presidential Library and Museum in Boston. Sketches made by this artist can be found in the very building they are meant to outline. Glass pyramids, big or small, are a central theme in his work. For 10 points, name this Chinese-American architect who died at 102 and designed the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame, the Louvre Pyramid, and the East Building of the National Gallery of Art.

One of these items from Uglich was reportedly exiled for its involvement in the riot about the death of Tsarevich Dmitry. Some of these items from Danilov were returned in 2008 from Harvard’s Lowell House. One of these items called “Tsar” created by the Motorin family was broken in a 1737 fire. These items are necessary to perform the trezvon, perebor, perezvon, and blagovest. The (*) clappers of these items are generally operated manually in Russia, but freely elsewhere. A building built to hold these items is the tallest part of the Kremlin. These items, which may be arranged in a carillon, are used to announce funerals and indicate the beginnings of church services. For 10 points, name these large percussion instruments often rung during Eastern Orthodox services.

In this play, Willie proclaims that he was accepted at Harvard with the same cordiality that Longfellow would have shown to a black woman dancing on Brittle Street. Two characters in this play joke about how they could’ve killed each other in the Boer War. One character remarks “’tis cool beneath thy willow trees” after stating that “the days grow hot, O Babylon.” A character in this play walks outside after (*) twenty years but comes back indoors after supposedly dodging an automobile. Guilt-ridden for turning in his mother who led “the Movement,” Don Parritt throws himself off the fire escape at the end of this play. In this play, Hickey admonishes other people about following “pipe dreams” before admitting to killing his wife. For 10 points, name this play about people residing in Harry Hope’s saloon, a work by Eugene O’Neill.

Founded through a 1970 merger of East and South Houses, this residence comprises six brick halls originally built as dormitories for Radcliffe College students. Its residential quadrangle was part of the first integration of male Harvard College students into Radcliffe housing as part of a co‑residential initiative, and its communal green space distinguishes it from Houses closer to the Charles River. (*) This House was originally called “South House” until being renamed for two significant benefactors of Harvard and Radcliffe. It contains a namesake cafe, where students can enjoy treats and beverages made by their peers from 8PM to 1AM. A housing day tradition, members of this house chant to incoming residents "you just won the lottery" after spelling the residential community's five-letter name. For 10 points, name this Harvard College "Quad" House whose mascot is a fish.

This residence incorporates former “Gold Coast” private dormitories such as Claverly and Westmorly, structures that predate the House system and were originally built with private baths, steam heat, and elaborate woodwork. It includes a building originally constructed in 1760 for an Anglican clergyman. That building, Apthorp House, is now the official residence for Faculty Deans of this house. Its linked common spaces feature a domed, tiled entrance known as the “Gold Room.” (*) Franklin D. Roosevelt’s 1900–1904 Harvard living quarters at this residence were restored as the only Presidential memorial at Harvard. For 10 points, name this "most historic" Harvard undergraduate house represented by the acorn.

Occupying a triangular site along Memorial Drive by the Charles River, this residence was part of Harvard’s original Harkness‑funded House building plan. Its ornate Baroque‑inspired stonework, gated entrance, and traditional dining hall draw comparisons to English collegiate architecture. (*) Known for its dark wood paneling, this house's dining hall is the only upperclassman servery that stays open after 7:30 in the evening. This house, named after Harvard's first president, is a popular cover image on admissions materials advertising Harvard College. For 10 points, name this River House known for its moose mascot.

The name of the dwarf planet previously known as "Planet X" was inspired in part by the initials of an astronomer associated with this House, whose early 20th‑century work on planetary orbits predicted the existence of Pluto. This House hosts one of New England’s longest-running student opera companies, producing full operas entirely staged and directed by undergraduates. Its dining hall walls feature portraits of family members including a Pulitzer‑winning poet and various other members of the house's namesake Boston Brahmin family. (*) From 1998 to 2018, the faculty deans of this House were the first same-sex couple to serve as deans of any Harvard College residence. This house is best known for its blue-capped tower containing a set of Russian bells rescued from the Danilov Monastery in Moscow, whose notes are rung to mark special occasions and echo across the Charles River. For 10 points, name the first and best undergraduate house at Harvard.

This residence was formed in 1931 by combining two 1913 freshman dormitories along Mill Street, making it one of Harvard’s original River Houses. Its namesake family includes colonial governors, and its design draws heavily from English Baroque architecture, with brick façades, stone trim, and a stepped entrance. The House underwent major renovations in the 2010s, which added an all-suite wing, restored historic woodwork, and expanded the fifth-floor rooftop common room overlooking the Charles River. (*) Its dining hall retains its original black-and-white checkerboard marble floors and vaulted wood ceiling, giving it one of the most visually distinctive dining spaces among Harvard undergraduate Houses. Notable alumni include John F. Kennedy, Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney, and several Pulitzer Prize winners who lived here as undergraduates. For 10 points, name this Harvard College undergraduate residential House located along the Charles River, represented by the lion mascot.

This house’s low-rise section includes a pottery studio, wood‑turning studio, and art gallery, one of only two such residential art spaces at Harvard. It hosts annual themed events in its dining hall, including the now-famous “Lather” foam party. (*)  Notable alumni of this house include political philosopher Cornel West and comedian Conan O'Brien. Its nineteen-story Brutalist concrete tower makes it the tallest and most visually distinctive undergraduate house at Harvard. In this house, students are famously guaranteed single-occupany housing for all three years of their upperclassman residency. For 10 points, name this "good" Harvard undergraduate house overlooking the Charles River, represented by the gorilla.

Opened in 1970 and built on the former Radcliffe Quadrangle, this residence’s four towers are each named after distinguished Radcliffe alumnae such as Barbara Tuchman and Mary Bingham. Its motto, Timete Arborem, is a reference to this House's inanimate mascot. One coveted suite in this house is called the 'Ten‑Man' and surrounds Harvard’s largest in‑suite common room. (*) It is the only Harvard undergraduate house named after a woman. A central plant‑lined fountain in this house's dining hall has made it known among students for its communal atmosphere, and its large “Fishbowl” common space includes a big‑screen projector and surround‑sound system installed in a major renovation. For 10 points, name this Harvard "Quad" house whose shield bears a tree.

This House was formed in 1970 by merging four former Radcliffe College dormitories: Comstock, Holmes, Moors, and Wolbach. One building in this house contains a distinctive bell tower suite, a large multi‑room living space with expansive views that was once the site where Mark Zuckerberg met his future wife as undergraduates. (*) Residents of this house maintain the tradition of “Pf-ing” words in house culture, creating nicknames like “PfD Hall” and “Pffamily” for common spaces and social groups. This house is also home to the Quad Grill, the only late-night student-run eatery in the Radcliffee Quadrangle. For 10 points, name this "Pfohome" to polar bears on Harvard's campus.

During the academic year, Harvard University Dining Services serves approximately 3,400 meals in this building each day, the most of any campus servery. Upperclassmen at Harvard College can only utilize their meal plan swipes to eat in this building's main servery during breakfast hours, although they can pick up to-go lunchtime meals in its basement "Flyby" room. (*) The basement of this building includes the Queen’s Head Pub, a former student-run alcohol venue that now functions as a space for college-run events and as the Harvard-Yale game ticket will-call. This building comprises one section of a 9,000‑square‑foot Gothic‑inspired hall that features hammerbeam trusses, vaulted ceilings, walnut paneling, and stained glass windows that pay tribute to scholars, writers, and military figures, giving it an aesthetic often compared to the Great Hall of Harry Potter. For 10 points, name this freshman dining hall at Harvard College, lovingly referred to as "Berg" by its first-year frequenters.

This course cannot be taken for credit if COMPSCI 1090A, COMPSCI 1090B, APCOMP 209A, APCOMP 209B, STAT 109A, or STAT 109B has already been completed. No prior experience in computer science or programming is required for enrollment in this course, which serves as an alternative to CS50 for fulfilling introductory computer science requirements at Harvard. The textbook for this course is written by Professor Michael Smith and features frequent references to his golden retriever, Cosmo. (*) Academic materials in this course are split into three "acts" increasing in difficulty and culimating in a comprehensive final project. This popular course taught at Harvard College is currently led by Professor Kitty Ascrizzi. For 10 points, name this Harvard undergraduate course that teaches computational thinking and programming, for which the script you are currently running was created.
Answer: CS32: Computational Thinking and Problem Solving;CS32;CompSci32;CS 32;CompSci 32

Question Text: This organization’s archives include early issues printed on a hand-operated press in 1876, and its members once staged a 1950s “kidnapping” of the Sacred Cod from the Massachusetts State House as a publicity stunt. In the 1960s, its national offshoot helped launch a humor brand that later produced Animal House and Vacation. (*) This group’s headquarters, often referred to as a "castle," includes a mock-Ivy-covered brick building topped with a small copper ibis, and is the site of initiation rituals historically involving elaborate, often secretive pranks. Its alumni include Conan O'Brien and Colin Jost, and it publishes a long-running undergraduate humor magazine at Harvard. For 10 points, name this semi-secret Harvard publication.

In one episode of this series, a character’s Harvard master’s degree in Folklore and Mythology is belittled for being "wacky." Another subplot in this show shows a character attempting to win a Harvard diploma from a claw machine, while in a different episode, a Harvard satellite is depicted spying on a student after she receives a failing grade. (*) In another gag, this show’s ultra-violent cartoon-within-a-show is jokingly said to be written by Harvard graduates, a self-referential nod to this series’ own writing staff. That cartoon is called the Itchy & Scratchy Show. Numerous writers for this show, including Conan O'Brien, graduated from Harvard and incorporated references to the university into episodes during the show’s early “golden age.” For 10 points, name this long-running animated series that features a family living in Springfield, including characters like Lisa, Bart, Marge, and Homer.

In 1968, students disrupted this event by occupying the field in protest of the Vietnam War. Other interruptions to this annual matchup have included elaborate “hacks” by neighboring MIT students, most famously when they replaced the Harvard Stadium scoreboard’s VE-RI-TAS lettering with the inflamatory phrase "HU-GE-EGO," drawing national attention and cementing their reputation for audacious pranks. Legendary games include a 29–29 tie and last-minute touchdowns that became part of Ivy League lore. (*) Tailgating traditions and alumni pilgrimages accompany this event, which is held in Cambridge, New Haven, and, occassionally, Boston's Fenway Park. For 10 points, name this annual sporting event.
Answer: The Game;Harvard-Yale;Harvard Yale;The Harvard-Yale Game;The Harvard Yale Game;Harvard-Yale Game;Harvard Yale Game;Yale-Harvard;Yale Harvard;The Yale-Harvard Game; The Yale Harvard Game;Yale-Harvard Game;Yale Harvard Game

Question Text: A recent Bollywood movie titled after this two word phrase has the year “2050” appended to its title. In one song with this title, the singer refers to herself as a “scarlet letter”. A film with this title sees a Harvard Law student struggle with his wife’s cancer and his relationship with his father, Oliver Barrett III. In the aforementioned song, the singer commands, “Romeo, take me somewhere we can be alone,” and exhorts its addressee to “just say yes.” For 10 points, name this phrase that titles a Taylor Swift song and a drama that says that the title emotion “means never having to say you’re sorry.”

A man who tried to steal one of these objects from Harvard in 1969 was foiled when he was knocked unconscious falling from a window. One of these objects now in Tokyo’s Keio University was briefly stolen from Berkeley in 1982. One of these objects known as Number 45 was bought from the heir of the Worcestershire sauce business by Estelle Doheny for $72,000 in 1950 and sold to a Japanese company in 1987 for $5.4 million. A museum named for the creator of these objects contains the (+) Shuckburgh example of them. These objects are sometimes named for Cardinal Mazarin, as the first complete example of one was found in Mazarin’s archive. A 2008 exhibition allowed visitors to see the differences between the three of these objects at the (*) Morgan, including one decorated by the Fust Master. Later editions of these objects left space for manual rubrication rather than using printed headings. These objects are sometimes known as B42 for the number of lines per page in them. For 10 points, name this early printed text by a man who set up a printing press in Mainz.
Answer: Gutenberg Bible;The Gutenberg Bible;Mazarin Bible;42-line Bible

Question Text: This justice may have been nominated to the Supreme Court after the president watched him give a speech at Harvard titled "The Soldier's Faith." An article by Zechariah Chafee criticizing an opinion by this justice may have influenced his dissent in Abrams v. US. In his dissent on a case concerning the constitutionality of the Bakeshop Act, this justice declared, "the Fourteenth Amendment does not enact Mr. Herbert Spencer's Social Statics." (*) This dissenter in Lochner v. New York introduced the "clear and present danger" test in Schenck v. US, where he wrote that free speech would not protect someone "shouting fire in a crowded theater." For 10 points, name this associate justice of the Supreme Court and son of a famous poet.
Answer: Oliver Wendell Holmes Jr.;Oliver Wendell Holmes;Holmes;Oliver Wendell Holmes, Jr.

Question Text: In a novel with this title, the protagonist asks, “What do we do with this, hang ourselves?” upon receiving an ethernet cable on move-in day at Harvard. Selin later uses that cable to email her boyfriend Ivan in that novel with this title. After an author swore off art and culture while living in Florence, he wrote a novel with this title which declares that Europe is “one big fantasy,” and that “beauty will save the world.” Elif Batuman’s only novel has this title, as does a novel whose protagonist accidentally smashes a (*) Chinese vase while denouncing the Catholic Church in one of his many rants. That novel of this title ends after a man stabs Nastasya Filippovna in the heart and begins with its protagonist emerging Christ-like from a Swiss sanatorium after treatment for epilepsy. For 10 points, give this title of a novel by Fyodor Dostoyevsky about the simple-minded Prince Myshkin.

This man and Samuel Warren wrote an article denouncing "snapshot photography" and its weakening of "moral standards." He was the driving force behind Massachusetts's Savings Bank Insurance League, a reform-minded attempt at providing life insurance. This man's dissent in Olmstead v. United States drew upon an article he wrote for the Harvard Law Review literally titled "The Right to Privacy." Along with Justices Cardozo and Stone, he was one of the "Three Musketeers" that opposed the Four Horsemen. The upholding of a law restricting women's working hours in the case of Muller v. Oregon relied heavily on this man's namesake brief. For 10 points, name this first Jewish Supreme Court justice.

The Brookings Institute outlined this program’s details in a 20-page report compiled at the request of Senator Arthur Vandenberg. This program overrode an earlier plan proposed by the Secretary of Treasury Henry Morgenthau. This initiative was announced in a Harvard commencement speech given by its namesake. The (*) Soviet Union and the Eastern Bloc rejected this program and instead developed the Molotov Plan. By 1951, almost $13 billion were spent on this program, the largest beneficiary of which was the United Kingdom. For 10 points, name this American economic aid program to Western Europe following World War II.
Answer: The Marshall Plan;Marshall Plan;The European Recovery Program;European Recovery Program;the ERP;ERP

Question Text: Variations in this material’s “headers” and “stretchers” form different kinds of “bonds.” At the University of Virginia, Thomas Jefferson used this material in a “serpentine” pattern. Christopher Wren inserted a cone of this material between the inner and outer domes of St. Paul’s Cathedral. This is the most prominent material in the Federal-style (*) Georgian architecture of Harvard University. Skyscrapers in this material include the Chrysler and Wainwright buildings. This material, along with stone and concrete blocks, is a typical component of masonry. For 10 points, name this material usually consisting of rectangular blocks of fired clay laid in mortar.

In a short story by this man, the protagonist is rejected by Yale for appearing too old, but later joins Harvard when he is almost 30 years older. In another of this writer’s short stories, a girl takes revenge on her cousin Marjorie by chopping off her braids. Meyer Wolfsheim claims to have fixed the 1919 World Series in a novel by this author, in which Tom Buchanan’s lover Myrtle is run over by Tom's wife Daisy, who is driving her lover Jay’s car. “The Curious Case of Benjamin Button” and “Bernice Bobs Her Hair” are by, for 10 points, what author of The Great Gatsby?
Answer: F Scott Fitzgerald;Francis Scott Fitzgerald;F. Scott Fitzgerald; Fitzgerald

Question Text: This man argued that consciousness is more fluid than a chain of ideas as previously described, thus coining the phrase “stream of consciousness”. (*) This Harvard professor famously contrasted the "healthy-minded temperament" among some zealots with the "sick soul" of others. He is famous for creating one of the first psychology labs in the United States and is the namesake for the Harvard University "Hall" housing many psychology labs to this day. For 10 points, name this American professor of philosophy and psychology, who wrote "The Will to Believe" and Pragmatism.

One character in this novel believes only courage and shrewdness are required to be successful, the former of which he has and the latter of which he can acquire. Another character in this novel nails himself in his attic and starves to death after his store is looted. After putting down a slave rebellion in Haiti, a man in this novel returns to the United States and gets a hundred miles of land from a drunken man. (*) Shreve narrates parts of this novel set in a cold Harvard bedroom. Henry murders Charles Bon to prevent him from marrying Judith in this novel narrated by Rosa Coldfield and Quentin Compson. For 10 points, name this biblically titled novel about the life of Thomas Sutpen, a work of William Faulkner.
Answer: Absalom, Absalom!; Absalom, Absalom; Absalom Absalom;Absalom Absalom!

Question Text: A notable academic paper about these animals begins with the line “This is the history of a crackpot idea.” A Harvard-MIT prank allegedly involved a man in a striped shirt feeding these animals. Nikola Tesla nursed a sick one of these animals and claimed he “loved her as a man loves a woman, and she loved me.” (*) A 1948 study induced “superstitions” in these animals which included turning around and bobbing their heads. One psychologist taught these animals to play ping-pong, and planned to have them guide missiles in Project Orcon. That man was B. F. Skinner. For 10 points, name these city-dwelling birds.
